speaker: interviewer
text: That should be-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you see the card?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I'll remember it anyway, but still.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to keep this with everything--  [SIGHS] can you hold it too--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so I don't get mixed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that should not be seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I might--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you want that right there, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I might need the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And that's why.

speaker: interviewee
text: I won't see if it's too far. No, the next ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. She can.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Scott, I don't know if that should be holding it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would need it for-- [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Scott Bryce, S-C-O-T-T, B-R-Y-C-E. Today's date is November 2, 1995. I'm privileged to conduct an interview with Lidia Eichenholz, and-- wow. Can you cut that and do it again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Scott Bryce, S-C-O-T-T, B-R-Y-C-E. Today's date is November 2, 1995. I am privileged to conduct an interview with Dr. Lidia Eichenholz. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Standby. We're ready to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Eichenholz, would you say and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. E-I-C-H-E-N-H-O-L-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lidia-- L-I-D-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name at birth, and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gilburd-- G-I-L-B-U-R-D

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would be 72 in July.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the city of your birth? Would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is Lwow-- L-V-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I may interrupt.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's eastern Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You might wanna-- that Lidia should always talk--

speaker: interviewee
text: To him.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Scott. And pretend that I'm not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do we do that again, or just go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. May I call you Lidia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell us something about your childhood in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in Lwow, which was second large city in Poland before the war. And I lived there till I was 6-years-old. And then my-- we are very prosperous people. My father study abroad for quite some time, but then returned back to Poland. Matter of fact, he even took part in the World War I. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- let's, if we could-- talk a little bit more about your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm coming to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we moved to-- then we had to move to Rowno. It's a medium-sized town in-- in eastern Poland, in Volhynia. And my childhood was very happy. We were-- we were-- own a lot of real estate, which was inherited. And my parents were very loving and caring-- whatever I-- they consider good for me. I had a younger brother who was born in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph. I had many relatives [BACKROUND NOISE] who live in this town, because that was the town when my mother was born. My father was born in Dubno, which is a small town 100 miles north of Rowno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowno. And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Solomon--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or Simon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Giilburd. Her maiden name was Gimberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gimberg

speaker: interviewee
text: G-I-M-B-- B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: B-E-R-G. Well, you indicated that you had a-- a wonderful childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We live in a very-- in-- in a very nice house, which had a large garden. We had all the conveniences. And I attended very good schools and had many-- many friends to play, many relatives to visit. So I-- so I consider myself a very lucky. Everybody was actually envious how well we are doing as a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your religious upbringing when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents belonged to synagogue in Rowno. But-- I-- and in school, we had to the-- religious the-- the religion-- every minority had its own religion class. And I had all the very good teachers. So I learned a lot about history of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that take place in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: That take place in a school. And then I read on a subject late at home, because the teacher was so stimulating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- was the teacher Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes I even remember his name. His name was Melamed, which means teacher in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, in-- were you involved in-- in Jewish rituals at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Holy days were celebrated, like fall holiday and-- and Passover-- also Purim. Hanukkah and Purim were celebrated, which is coming now in-- in February. Most-- most of the time was a big celebration. It is a very oriental holiday, which is now celebrated in Israel more than in this country. It involves a famous story of Holocaust, which it did not take place in ancient Persia. And Jewish people were safe, because the queen, was-- her name is Hadassah, or Esther. She has-- she was a-- a very philanthropic person. And she had a lot of influence. She herself was Jewish. There's a long story about-- I would say she was the first woman feminist in the history [LAUGHS] of-- of Jewish people. That's why the many hospitals are called Hadassah, because of her charity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had Shabbat at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had. In-- it was not over done. But just the pleasant part of Shabbat took place, like lighting the candle, and having a nice meal, inviting guests, or relatives. So the table was always very festive, with the fish and the wine-- gefilte fish, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about the size and makeup of the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rowno happened to be one of the city in Poland which had more Jewish people than any other. It was-- it had about-- maybe 70% of population in Rowno was Jewish. It was very prosperous. It was south of Pinsk, which is better known as a Zionist city. But the money came always from Rowno. That's why it had such good schools, which I enjoy too. We-- I-- and I think my school years were very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to talk a little bit about the community and how you related to the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went-- of course, I had many, many relatives and friends. So I was always busy with them. But my father always felt that you should establish contacts with non-Jewish people. And he try-- especially in sports. He was very interested in gliders and flying. And-- and that brought others as participant. Rowno was actually one of these cities-- were awfully-- were-- which suffered most during the war. The first killing of Jews took place in Rowno, because those are very ferocious gestapo men. Alfred Rosenberg, he was stationed in Rowno. And-- and about this time of the year, like beginning of November, most of the Jewish people in Rowno were-- were exterminated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get back a little bit to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To my--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your move and your childhood from Lwow to Rowno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved when I was 6, because my grandfather died. And-- and we had a big real estate in the Rowno. So we had to move. And we lived there till '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father basically was a mechanical engineer, motors of internal combustion. He study a lot abroad in Belgium. And I think as I mentioned before, he took part in-- in-- he fought combat. I have even pictures in uniform-- on a Belgian side--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: --during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he went to a boarding school in Belgium-- actually, in Lowicz. That's an-- that-- that's a school where had a French university. It just opened. And they recruited him. I don't know which-- how they knew about those cities in eastern Poland. Nevertheless-- which was actually, that was even before the World War I. But they recruited their people who could pay high tuition. And they have to be boarded. They had to go early, because they had to be boarded with families before. So they learn to speak French almost without accent. A number of people from Rowno and Dubno went there. Many trained as a doctors. And they later went to Congo, to Belgian Congo, as a doctors and did quite well-- at least survived the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father came back--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland,

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: --after the World War I. He married and settled back in a-- in a-- in-- actually, after he got married, they moved to Lwow. He-- he married a

speaker: interviewer
text: To--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a girl from Rowno. But they moved [LAUGHS] to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to Lwow. Did he do engineering work? Or did he manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: He tried for-- he tried for a while, but he could not get a good-- eastern Poland was not industrialized. He could not get a good jobs. And so he went into textile business, which is-- which was a very profitable at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the main business of the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, many people were in textile. You know, as you know, they had to buy the textiles in Lodz, or Bialystok, or Bielsko. They were old textile centers. Lodz was of course, the bigger one. And they also provided many-- were many people who did all the kind of other jobs, like-- like constructions or partisan-- or jobs like shoe-- there were a lot of shoemakers--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and tailors-- all kind of trade people live in Rowno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit your father's works while you were a child, where he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had several textile business, mostly wholesale. He had it in-- in several towns around Rowno. Eventually, he moved his main business to Rowno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was very successful in this. And that was till '39, when the Russians came, when the Poland was divided between Germany and Russia. And then he had to go back to his engineering. [LAUGHS] And we had to leave Rowno and move to Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about going to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the school was-- I started grade school, which was public. And then opened a new school, which is called Oswiata, O-S-W-I-A-T-A. That was a private school, but had all the right of a-- a government school. And I started it when I was 10. And that was a very, very good school-- a third-- a lot of people from surrounding area. A matter of fact, they were so full, they had to run-- they never own a building. They always [LAUGHS] rent it, because director felt that all the money should go into the teachers. And building can wait. And the school became so successful, that we had to in three shifts-- morning, [LAUGHS] afternoon, [LAUGHTER] and evening. It provided many foreign-- we had many good foreign language teachers, and-- although religion was taught as a history [BACKROUND NOISE] of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any Jewish organizations while in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, we did not-- I belonged to all the organizations which were in the school. But I-- that was about it. My brother, my younger brother, tried to be a shomer, which is for young boys, a Zionist organization. But we had many-- many organizations which were in the school. And there was no more time to join anything outside. I was too young anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- what language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I spoke several languages. It was a extended family. And we all lived together, or very close together. So I spoke-- but older generation, like my grandparents-- I spoke Russian or sometimes Yiddish. And then-- and then my-- my parents slowly were switching to Polish, which is not so different from Russian, except for the alphabet. So I spoke mostly Polish in school, because Polish was the language of instruction. But I start slowly speaking Polish with my parents as the years went by. I also-- we had also many relatives from Germany, and they came to Poland once Hitler came to power. And they lived with us for a while till they found a place. So I-- so I learned from them a lot of German, because they spoke mostly German.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Languages came easy to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I never thought that I should take a-- a class or anything. Although, school offers several languages that I had to take. Like Hebrew was taught in school as a foreign language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I heard about. I personally did not feel it much, because we were trying-- I was very protected and sheltered. And our school had very good reputation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, non-Jews were of course-- there was-- there was the population, Jewish people, which were majority, then Ukrainian people, and also Polish people, which were in administration in teaching. Before the war, I did not-- and I-- when I went still to-- to public school, I did not feel much of any anti-Semitism. You had to compete-- where there-- you had to-- my family wasn't Orthodox. So it was easier for me to compete with them. My language was good-- and of course, amount of prestige my family had. And there is so much said now on anti-Semitism in Poland. But I-- I-- I-- I-- I knew that it existed, but it never came very close to me. The maids in our house or many people that my father employed were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you think of any experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We used to travel. And some experiences-- well, we used to go to swim to a lake. And-- and that apparently was site of anti-Semitism, because you had to compete with-- with those, let's say, country boys, or older boys, who are good swimmer. But I learned to swim quite early. So they never touch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were older, and in the school, what did you hear about, or know about, the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did hear a lot. We hear about Crystal-- Crystal Night. We hear about actually Jewish people who had Polish citizenship were expelled from Germany. They had to go back to Poland. We-- and we had some of those people, but although they told us certain things, we did not believe, or were very skeptical about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember some experiences in which you talked about with your classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we got some families which came from Germany. The kids joined the school. And they taught us about, but we thought that we are protected, especially when the war became-- starts readying. We felt that Chamberlain will-- will do everything for us. A matter of fact, I write this-- I wrote a short story how-- how-- how much kids and adults were mistaken or had delusion that nothing will happen to us. Although on this time, there was a new party in Poland. Pilsudki died. That was Marshal Pilsudski. He died in '36. And the new government, the man who took over was not-- he was a rather tolerant man. But the new man was not that tolerant, so-- although he not had that much prestige or power. So there was a new party-- a-- national party to the-- to the right, which start spreading anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- and I heard about plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you travel around Poland as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I did with my mother. We went every summer to a different place in mountains or in the sea. Ironically, we had friends in Sopot and Danzig, and we visited them. But that was very-- that was only a couple of years before the war. But we never-- they eventually left Poland. And-- but when the adults were speaking, I as a child, was excluded. And I did not realize all those things, although I read books on the subject which were written in Germany but translated into Polish. And they were quite open.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see any of the disturbances in Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not, because that was summer season where the tourists came. So I was very-- I was just strike by a comparison to Poland how much Danzig and Sopot was prosperous. And-- and everybody spoke German. Polish was kind of looked down. That was my first experience in Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were many Germans in Danzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, every-- every-- the whole population was German. They were-- there was some Polish people. But I have not met them, except they work in a-- restaurants or something, usually in lower position. But there was apparently, that they found out after the war, there was a-- some-- or this post office in Danzig. It was given to-- which I saw-- where describe in [NON-ENGLISH]-- It was made also to a movie. He's from Danzig. And that was under the Polish administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of books did you read as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had many-- many books were just by Polish writers. But some were translated, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: Into--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from-- from German to Polish. We had actually a lot of Scandinavian writers, because they would get translated into this-- into the German. And then immediately Poland had many translators. So I read all about this-- [NON-ENGLISH], about PP. And there's a Danish writer, which is quite famous-- Karin Michaelis. And she wrote a very good children's series, which we read all that. And of course, we read It-- it's-- it was translated from German by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kastner.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Kastner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. And Emil-- and there were-- that had a sentence-- Eric Kastner. That had sentences. But they have not mentioned anti-Semitism, or any-- or very little was said about rise of national socialism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read any-- you read religious books too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were not too many actually, available. And the woman did not participate that much in religious serious as they do nowadays. The synagogue was still segregated. And the men sit downstairs, and the woman sit upstairs and eating. It was preparing a food and preparing of Shabbas. It was the main occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more question, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you-- did you visit Warsaw or some of the big cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We had relatives and friends in Warsaw before the war, just much smaller than it is now. And I enjoyed very much. It had a good Jewish theater, which actually came to Rowno with a very famous actress-- Ida Kaminska. And they put many old Jewish plays. I also saw It's a very famous book by Ansky. The man written still in 19th century. Dybbuk means a person who is obsessed. That was made to a movie. And I like it very much. And it was scary, [LAUGHS] because it ends badly. And we saw some movie even-- even a bit-- Molly Picon made here, but they were given in Poland. Molly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Molly--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Picon, P-I-C-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Picon.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in New York-- Atkins. I saw her in life when I came to United States. She's dead now.

speaker: interviewer
text: We should change.
